## 7. Technology comparison for pangenomics

No sequencing technology is best for every pangenomics project. Start with the biological question, then choose the data type that provides the required accuracy, molecule span, throughput, and long-range context.

| Question | Strong data type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Many samples, common SNPs | Short reads | Scalable, accurate, cost-effective. |
| De novo assembly | PacBio HiFi, often plus Hi-C | Accurate long reads produce contiguous assemblies; Hi-C helps chromosome scaffolding. |
| Very large repeats or telomere-to-telomere goals | Ultra-long nanopore plus complementary data | Long molecules can span repeats that other reads cannot. |
| Structural variant discovery | Long reads | Reads can span breakpoints and inserted sequence. |
| Phasing | Long reads, linked reads, trio data, Hi-C | Phase requires information connecting variants across distance. |
| Methylation linked to haplotype | Native long-read sequencing | Genetic and epigenetic information can be observed on the same molecule. |
| Isoform discovery | Long-read RNA/cDNA sequencing | Full-length transcript evidence reduces ambiguity. |
| Graph genotyping at scale | Short reads against a graph, or long reads for complex loci | Graphs reduce linear-reference bias; long reads help complex regions. |

---
